data.title: Modiv Industrial Sits In The Crosshairs Of Tariffs
data.description:
The past several months have set the stage to materially change trading norms around the globe. As tariffs take hold, the economy is trying to make heads and tails of a new reality. Sitting center stage in these changes in Modiv Industrial (NYSE:MDV), a net lease REIT that acts as a sale leaseback partner for operators of industrial real estate.

In November, I initiated coverage on MDV providing an overview of the company’s portfolio, business model, and short operating history. The article was cautionary, focusing on the risks of sale leaseback investing.

The purpose of today’s article is to follow up, review the recent decline since our Sell rating, and discuss a deteriorating outlook amidst speed bumps to domestic investment and a looming trade conflict. Without further ado…

Who Is Modiv Industrial?

In my prior coverage of MDV, I provided an update on the company’s portfolio, including the asset count and annualized base rent.

MDV’s portfolio consists of 43 properties generating an annualized base rent of $40.2 million. The properties cover 4.5 million square feet, meaning each property averages around 100,000 square feet.

MDV owns 39 industrial assets that account for their core portfolio. These assets account for 77% of ABR. The remaining portion are generated from non-core assets, which includes a KIA auto dealership in Los Angeles and several other non-industrial assets that the company is actively working on turning over.

Based on the most recent filings from the company, the portfolio has not changed significantly. As of the end of 2024, MDV owned 43 operating properties, comprised of 39 industrial properties and four noncore assets. Looking deeper, the largest industry is infrastructure, with 17 properties accounting for nearly 25% of annualized base rent and 28% of portfolio square footage. The next two largest categories are automotive and industrial products, accounting for 15% and 14% of annualized based rent, respectively.

The company's largest individual geography is California, accounting for nearly $12 million in annualized base rent, or 30% at the portfolio level.

Review of Prior Coverage

In November, I initiated coverage on MDV. The purpose of the article was to introduce the business, discuss the company’s strategy, and outline key risks that I felt the market was possibly overlooking in the valuation of MDV. While I clearly did not believe that MDV was headed for bankruptcy with any imminence, I rated the company a Sell.

A large piece of my prior coverage centered around MDV’s sale leaseback heavy business model. Like many net lease REITs, MDV acts as a capital partner for private equity sponsored businesses. They act as a piggy bank to buy the real estate from companies that are either growing, or more likely the case for industrial companies, recapitalizing. These aging businesses are often conservatively capitalized and generationally owned, giving PE an opportunity to strip away the assets, lever up, and provide healthy returns to their shareholders all while reducing their risk.

Part of my thesis is below:

MDV needs time to prove itself, mature their business, and establish a portfolio worth buying…. However, I would not have an interest in being a shareholder given the inherent risk embedded in their business. MDV earns a Sell rating.

The publication ruffled more feathers than expected. From my perspective, MDV was facing risks associated with tenant creditworthiness and exposure to economic changes from the incoming administration. In the meantime, MDV continued to climb and outperform the net lease sector.

Since publication, shares of MDV have fallen by 13.3%. Although the shares have declined with a degree of consistency since November, the announcement of recent tariffs have been the nail in the coffin for the already volatile market. The recent decline has changed MDV’s pricing, pushing the yield to over 8%. From one perspective, this presents a different opportunity set than just five or six months ago, with higher return through the dividend and greater potential for the company’s multiple to expand. However, I am not ready to accept such an optimistic view.

Once again, I believe the market is significantly underestimating the potential impact of President Trump’s economic policies to certain industrial REITs.

Economic Policy Could Hurt MDV

One thing is certain, times are changing quickly. President Trump has unleashed an arsenal of what I have been calling “novel” policies. This means policies and strategies with little historical precedence, which inherently increases uncertainty around the impacts of those policies. The tariff plan would qualify as one such policy that will certainly impact American business. Let’s explore the implication of some of these changes for MDV.

Over the past year, President Trump has expressed interest in either revoking or overhauling Biden’s CHIPS act. CHIPS stands for Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors.

What is CHIPS?

The CHIPS act was an economic initiative designed to accelerate investment in the United States semiconductor manufacturing. The incentive program was designed to encourage foreign investment in the United States and enhance our production capacity.

The Peterson Institute provides an overview of the act and its short five-year history.

While the US dominates much of the chip supply chain, by 2020 it held a small and shrinking share of semiconductor fabrication, with no production of advanced chips on American soil. US policymakers believed that attracting production to US territory would reduce the risk that virtually all the world’s advanced semiconductor manufacturing could be shut off in an invasion or blockade of Taiwan and would benefit US national security and hopefully lead to positive spillovers for the US economy and technological innovation. The CHIPS Act induced many of the world’s leading semiconductor companies to produce chips in the US by offering a generous advanced manufacturing tax credit of 25 percent to facilities that make semiconductors or equipment that makes chips

The primary takeaway from the short history of CHIPS is that it has been wildly successful. Over just several years, construction of electronics production facilities in the United States has increased by approximately tenfold, much of which can be linked to CHIPS.

The CHIPS Act has been largely hailed as a win. Demand for U.S. manufacturing space soared as the first round gave $39B in loans, and $220B in development was announced in its first year alone.

Despite the success of the CHIPS act, it appears as though the current administration is considering modifying scrapping the program entirely.

Trump May End CHIPS

Over the past year, President Trump has expressed interest in ending CHIPS in favor of tariffs.

President Donald Trump has pulled execution of the CHIPS Act under a new entity overseen by Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick, who reportedly intends to take a tougher position on the program meant to spur domestic semiconductor manufacturing.

Rather than incentivizing trade partners to invest inside of the United States, tariffs would punish American businesses for operating/investing outside of the United States for the supply chains. One could argue that these are two very different roads to arrive at the same location. Putting that aside, revocation of the act would be a considerable disruption for the ongoing wave of manufacturing facilities.

Ending the bill would not only disrupt the ongoing wave of construction but would likely reduce the effectiveness of any future government programs as trust is broken between administration changes. Instead, the current administration may be better off by devising legislation that would promote investment in targeted sectors of manufacturing, such as semiconductors. CHIPS was effective in bringing foreign investment to the United States, a significant economic driver. Accounting for the past five years of inflation, the act spurred over $100 billion in investment inside of the United States, more than the entire amount of manufacturing capacity produced in the United States over the previous decade.

Unlike tariffs, which are likely to have an inflationary impact on the price of goods, the CHIPS act was able to spur investment without raising costs directly while also increasing our competition with China. If the government eliminates incentive programs for domestic investment, foreign producers could see significantly reduced motivation to build inside of America.

Conclusion

As it relates to MDV, the business model of the company is heavily dependent on the growth trajectory of manufacturing businesses inside of the United States. Going further, irrespective of growth, the company depends on the manufacturing industry, which has been struggling to revitalize over the past decade. As the government unveils strong initiatives which are designed to foster manufacturing inside of America, disruption is almost certain to have an overall negative impact. For a capital partner like MDV, these disruptions can be significant enough to tenants to cause material issues which lead to rent relief or, in a worst-case scenario, bankruptcy.

The specialization of many facilities owned by MDV dictates that the company would likely have a difficult time releasing facilities as is. A more likely scenario would be an expensive scrape and redevelopment, which is costly and disadvantageous during a tumultuous market. With these risks in mind, the increasing risk factors associated with an investment in MDV are simply too great for the company to look attractive. While the recent declines in share price have made the company more attractive, the macroeconomic disruption and perky outlook for US manufacturing earns the company a Hold rating.

data.title: Playstudios: Few Positives Aside From Its Cheap Valuation
data.description:
Investment thesis

While Q4 results for Playstudios (NASDAQ:MYPS) were within management's guidance, the company's outlook for 2025 pointed to an expectation for lower revenue and adjusted EBITDA. While the stock is cheaply valued, especially with its net cash position accounting for 60% of the current market cap, when taking a look at key metrics related to its rewards platform, I am concerned with some fundamental issues I see. While I bring up certain positive aspects and potential upside catalysts, I am inclined to stick to the sidelines for now.

Low valuation reflects underlying risks and concerns

Based on the midpoint of management's FY25 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $50 million, shares are valued at an EV to adjusted EBITDA multiple of just 1.4, when accounting for the company's net cash position of $109 million. Notably, its net cash position represents 60% of its current market cap. This valuation multiple represents a large discount compared to a relevant peer like Playtika (PLTK) which has also struggled to generate organic growth and is valued at a mid-single digit adjusted EBITDA multiple.

While I recognize that further downside could be limited by the large net cash position and the business being consistently FCF positive, it is difficult to foresee major upside with the current guidance implying that this year would continue a similar trend since 2023 where both revenue and adjusted EBITDA have seen considerable year-over-year declines. Furthermore, since stock-based compensation, licensing costs and capex together represent more than $40 million annually, which are not included in the adjusted EBITDA figure, it is noteworthy that GAAP earnings are substantially lower.

I would also point out that while the company's management team has on several occasions remarked that its stock is grossly undervalued, including during the Q4 earnings call, there has been only an insignificant amount of capital allocated towards share repurchases in the last two quarters.

Besides the company's declining revenue and adjusted EBITDA figures, I would point to some fundamental issues associated with its Playawards platform, with the retail value of purchases being down 38% year over year in Q4, as shown above. Other metrics like Average Daily Active Users ("DAU") and Average Monthly Active Users ("MAU") also saw year-over-year declines of 19% and 14% respectively. While its CEO mentioned that this was due to an ongoing cleaning up of some of its platform mechanics, it remains unclear as to when this process will be completed and if the deterioration in these key metrics will continue going forward. These aspects definitely shake my confidence in the business, and its ability to drive user engagement and retention on its platform, especially as the company also no longer expects to be able to launch its Loyalty as a Service offering, which was previously touted as an opportunity to be a high margin revenue source.

Turning to other risks, the recent deterioration in the macroeconomy which has led to weaker consumer sentiment will also be an additional headwind that the company has to deal with.

Positive aspects and potential catalysts

Besides its low valuation, there are some positives which I believe could drive upside for investors. One such factor is the increasing number of in-app purchases conducted through its direct-to-consumer channel. This accounted for 8% of in-app purchase revenue in Q4, and it comes at significantly higher gross margins as it avoids app store fees. Here, based on what I've seen with other mobile gaming companies, I agree with management that this number could exceed 20% of in-app purchase revenue in the coming years.

While management also carried out a restructuring program in Q4 with the aim of reducing operating expenses, a major portion of the savings are planned for the development and marketing on its upcoming sweepstakes initiative, set to launch later this year. This is a key catalyst I see for the stock. The sweepstakes market has grown rapidly in recent years and the management estimates that the sweepstakes market is worth around $4.5 billion, something that is further validated by other market participants. While sweepstakes was not mentioned by management until the second half of last year, I would point out that a reference to it was made more than forty times during the last earnings call, thus highlighting its significance. Notably, management has not included any contribution from this initiative in current guidance, though they did mention that a successful rollout could lead to between $15 million to $30 million in revenue upside this year.

Lastly, given the company's large cash position and capacity to take on debt if needed, I do acknowledge that a transformative acquisition could be a potential upside catalyst that investors can look for.

Conclusion

Although the valuation of MYPS stock is extremely cheap, offering limited downside, I have walked through several risks and concerns related to the business and its outlook. While I have highlighted some positives and potential catalysts that could offer investors meaningful upside, it is too early for me to judge the likelihood of success and this should instead be monitored in the coming quarters. For now, I recommend staying away from the stock and therefore maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: PBDC: Valuation Reversion Makes BDCs A Sector Buy
data.description:
Since the beginning of the year, business development companies have found themselves at the heart of investor attention as volatility, changing interest rate expectations, and increasing uncertainty continue to shape the market. The President’s novel economic policies have caused increasing uncertainty across every corner of the global economy, and the markets are reeling.

BDCs, which primarily invest in middle market private companies, offer a blend of exposure to private credit and private equity. For most investors, these assets offer yield and diversification. Over the past several years, the rise of the asset class has been exceptional. However, as the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy now shows signs of change and the global economy stutters, BDC valuations have started to show signs of mean reversion that warrant attention.

Today’s publication from REITer’s Digest explores the current state of the business development company market, with an emphasis on valuation dynamics and mean reversion. Over the past several months, I have published several critical articles covering business development companies. This article will follow up on the thesis, which predicted a significant shift in valuations for some of the sector’s largest participants. As the sector shifts, we will explore the sector in its entirety via the Putnam BDC Income ETF (NYSEARCA:PBDC), the only actively managed exchange-traded fund of BDCs.

Fund Overview

In February, I initiated coverage on PBDC, one of the largest exchange-traded funds covering the sector. In fact, PBDC is one of the only funds covering BDCs, which are still considered a risky and niche asset class by many investors. However, I believe the long-term performance of some of the sector’s all stars warrant their consideration. For example, Gladstone Investment (GAIN), a smaller BDC and my personal favorite, has trounced the S&P 500 since the financial crisis by blending debt and equity investing.

My prior coverage of PBDC included a comprehensive overview of the fund's construction and active management process from the manager, Putnam. Included in the previous coverage was an analysis of the fund's assets under management period. PBDC still manages just $175,000,000 in assets, making the fund a smaller ETF.

Also clarified was an explanation of the fund's complicated expense ratio. Despite fund level expenses looking exorbitantly high as quoted on the top line, we uncover through the fund's annual report that the ETF charges just 75 basis points as a management fee and the remainder corresponds to fees charged by the fund's individual holdings.

Another primary consideration with funds like PBDC is the concentration risk associated with the business development company sector. The business development company sector has relatively few participants. Accordingly, the fund has only 24 holdings and the top five account for nearly 40% of the fund's total assets. The top 10 holdings of the fund account for more than 70% of assets.

That said, each individual business development company is thoroughly diversified, usually holding hundreds if not thousands of individual loans from a wide variety of different borrowers. Typically, in the world of business development companies and private credit, diversification is the name of the game and managers are best suited for spreading their risk as widely as possible across the economy.

The performance of the fund in the sector has also been strong. Despite recent losses, performance of the fund since inception remains positive and performance of some of the largest participants in the sector continues to power forward during the higher for longer era.

However, the past several weeks have put forth a serious speed bump for business development company investors. Participants across the market have encountered the same volatility which has struck some of the largest companies around the globe. Over a period of weeks, the business development company sector has fallen in line with the broader market, beginning to decline precipitously.

Let’s explore BDC valuations in more detail and explain the current change in pricing.

Valuation Reversion Strikes Again

Over the past six months, I have written several cautionary articles covering the BDC sector. Although I have rated the sector a buy throughout the rising interest rates and higher for longer eras, pricing has recently begun to deteriorate. The market turmoil has left no stone unturned, and the tide has gone out on nearly every asset class. This includes BDCs, despite being a resilient asset class. In fact, several of these articles that were published over the past two months are relevant as some of PBDC’s largest individual holdings.

At the beginning of February, I published an article covering Ares Capital (ARCC), rating the fund a Hold. Despite being a top performing BDC, ARCC was priced at nearly 1.2x book value and looked historically expensive on the basis of dividend and earnings yields. My thesis was simple:

To put it succinctly, ARCC has little room for upside, trading at a compressed yield and expanded price to book value.

Since publication, ARCC shares have declined by nearly 13% as the tide goes out on one of the market’s largest business development companies.

In the meantime, the company’s price to book ratio has now declined to 0.98x on a forward basis. With the fund’s valuation zoomed out, we’ll note that today’s price looks more aligned with the market’s historical valuation of the company.

Perhaps an even better example is Main Street Capital (MAIN). In February, I published a controversial article covering MAIN and rating the company a Sell.

The article was controversial, generating nearly 130 comments, many of whom were dissenters who disagreed with the rating. Although I was a long term MAIN investor, share prices had reached what I believe were an unreasonable valuation. In the article, I provided an explanation of my decision to sell my shares.

While MAIN continues to price at twice net asset value, both alternatives offer attractive risk-adjusted yields relative to MAIN. Should MAIN return to the midpoint valuation, an investment may be warranted. However, while MAIN’s valuation continues to sit well above historical averages and, from my perspective, logic. Taking profits looks to be a reasonable option. Especially when a yield within 100bps can be obtained via a money market fund with minimal risks and dollar in, dollar out pricing.

At publication, MAIN was trading at a price to net asset value of 2.0x, significantly above the company’s historical valuation. On one hand, MAIN’s equity holdings continue to perform well and likely imply some unincorporated gains that could make NAV slightly soft. Even still, pricing at double book value was too rich for a business development company like MAIN.

Since publication, shares of MAIN have declined by more than 15%, now sitting around $50 per share. As MAIN once again begins to stabilize, investors may note that the company’s forward price to book value is around 1.6x, in line with the company’s five-year rolling average according to Seeking Alpha.

On a combined basis, these two companies account for more than 15% of PBDC’s total holdings. Over the past several months, both funds have undergone a significant valuation reversion. Shares have fallen significantly year to date, reaching their historical valuation midpoints on a net asset value basis. From this perspective, BDCs look considerably more attractive than just six months ago.

Once again, there is a lesson to be learned that trees do not often grow to the sky. History has the potential to act as an anchor point for investor psychology.

Conclusion

From my perspective, it is this change in valuation at the market level which has begun to impact the pricing of business development companies. For a diversified fund which covers a material portion of the market’s participants, a significant decrease in share price is almost always caused by systemic risk, rather than individual impact. Systemic risk is certainly rising with the specter of a trade war growing and unrelenting turmoil in the markets. That said, the business development company sector is simply returning to a more reasonable point. PBDC is a relatively new fund with only several years of operating history.

Zooming out, we will quickly notice that PBDC has still been profitable for investors since the fund’s launch in 2022. Total return remains positive despite the recent declines. With interest rates remaining higher for longer and valuations looking attractive, PBDC earns a Buy rating.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/14/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: ClearBridge International Growth ACWI Ex-U.S. Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen, Michael Testorf, CFA, & Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

Broadening Participation Supports Outperformance

Market Overview

Led by a sharp revival in Europe, international equities delivered strong gains in the first quarter as increasing policy uncertainty in the U.S. spurred investors to look overseas for more predictable returns. The benchmark MSCI All Country World Ex-U.S. Index advanced 5.23%, outperforming its U.S. counterpart, the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500), by over 950 basis points in a reversal of global leadership. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index added 2.93%, boosted by a rebound in China.

Some might say this was a rally driven by necessity, as U.S. President Donald Trump’s aggressive and iconoclastic approach to global trade and foreign policy incited the European Union to action, although key policy leaders in Europe had been in discussion on the topic well beforehand. Global markets, including Europe, sold off sharply to start the second quarter after Trump unveiled a much wider and steeper than expected program of tariffs on all global trading partners, a situation expected to remain in flux in the near term.

As we detail in a new report on improving conditions for non-U.S. equities, Germany is leading a substantial increase in defense, infrastructure and overall fiscal spending as the Continent begins to shoulder greater responsibility for its security and economic growth. Performance has also been supported by easing price pressures, with inflation in the eurozone falling to 2.2% in March. Inflation in the region has now remained below 3% for 18 months, giving the European Central Bank greater latitude than the U.S. Federal Reserve in continuing to cut interest rates. The ECB has cut rates twice to start 2025 and, while growth has been tepid, the expansion is expected to remain intact.

Trump’s tariff actions have also sparked increased fiscal spending in China, with government officials billing generative AI as a key driver of the country’s economic growth and technology independence. Improving sentiment toward the private sector helped push the MSCI China Index up 15.02% during the quarter.

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Performance

While innovation and tech investment are becoming greater priorities in Europe, overseas growth stocks lagged for the quarter with the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. EAFE Growth Index up 1.96% while the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Value Index, home to many of the companies expected to lead a resurgence in manufacturing activity, surged 8.58%. Despite these value headwinds, the ClearBridge International Growth ACWI Ex-US Strategy outperformed.

New addition BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), the world’s largest EV maker, drove strength among our consumer discretionary holdings. China is becoming a more investable story due to government support of private industry. The Chinese EV market is being boosted by many local manufacturers with exciting and frequent new model introductions, an extensive network of 2 million charging stations with a universal single plug standard and simple, widely used charging payment platform. Six- to eight-year warranties for EVs should also facilitate consumer adoption.

Another bright spot was banks, with U.K.-based Lloyds, Spain’s Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria (BBVA) and Italy’s Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY)(OTCPK:IITSF) all rerating more than 30%. A sector that was neglected for more than decade, investors are beginning to rediscover European banks for their sustainable earnings growth at some of the lowest valuations with highest capital distributions globally.

In addition, the Strategy received diversified contributions within communication services, led by emerging growth holding Sea Limited (SE), a gaming, e-commerce and fintech provider, and structural growth name Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGF)(OTCQX:DTEGY). The latter was lifted by a jump in its majority owned T-Mobile US wireless franchise, which upgraded forward guidance and reported accelerating revenue growth and margin improvement in its latest quarterly results. T-Mobile’s stable capex, network quality and sustainable market share gains in both rural markets and in the enterprise segment continue to drive the best free cash flow growth among global telecoms.

Stock selection in health care and industrials held back portfolio performance with particular weakness among biopharmaceutical holdings Novo Nordisk (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF), Zealand Pharma (OTCPK:ZLDPF) and UCB (OTCPK:UCBJF). Novo, a leader in the blockbuster diabetes and obesity market, has been hurt recently by the rise of compounding pharmacies taking advantage of the shortage of Ozempic in the market. The shortage, now over, and the company’s more aggressive approach to dealing with them should allow for better performance moving forward. UCB, which specializes in neurology and immune diseases, has been a strong performer over the last year but sold off as the biotech markets have more generally come under pressure. We maintain confidence in the market positioning and outlook for these names.

Portfolio Positioning

We took advantage of the changing investment landscape to add 10 new positions in the quarter while exiting 11 others.

Defense spending is expected to increase dramatically in the EU and Japan, which should boost revenues for new additions Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF)(OTCPK:EADSY), the largest aerospace contractor in Europe with both a commercial airline and defense business, and Japan’s Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHI)(OTCPK:MHVYF)(OTCPK:MHVIY). The latter should benefit from exceptional growth in several of its business units. Gas turbines for electricity generation are seeing significant and prolonged growth due to increased power demand from data centers, electrification and reshoring of manufacturing. MHI is the largest defense contractor in Japan, where military spending could be increasing from 1% to 3% of GDP. Finally, MHI is a leading supplier to the Japanese nuclear power generation complex, where 20 of 33 reactors remain closed, though political consensus might warm up to restarting most of them as Japan’s electricity consumption grows.

Trane Technologies (TT), an Ireland-based HVAC supplier, was our largest addition to the portfolio. Trane has a sharp customer value proposition in both replacement and new installations as its products have improved energy efficiency meaningfully in the past five years, bringing significant energy cost savings and quick, top-quality paybacks for replacing old equipment. Avoidance of fines and penalties for non-compliance with pending energy efficiency regulations in both the U.S. and Europe are a motivation for sales while the company’s data center business is also growing at a rapid pace.

We also increased our financials exposure with the addition of banks NatWest (NWG)(OTCPK:RBSPF) and UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY)(OTCPK:UNCFF). Scotland-based NatWest has gone through a decade-long restructuring that included a government bailout following the Global Financial Crisis and is finally returning to normalcy. NatWest has a quality loan book and, with lending subdued the last several years, it has less bad debt on its balance sheet. It is the most interest rate sensitive of the large U.K, banks with net interest income, expected to increase over the next three years, poised as a major driver of future earnings. UniCredit, meanwhile, should benefit from a consolidating Italian banking market. Earnings should increase over the next two years, driven by rising fee income and effective cost management.

While Japan has become a growing area of interest for investors and we have strategically been reducing our underweight to the region, we want to own companies where we have high conviction. During the quarter, we exited four Japanese holdings to consolidate the portfolio in better opportunities. Running shoe maker Asics (OTCPK:ASCCF)(OTCPK:ASCCY) was sold as it hit our price target, while we exited medical equipment makers Olympus (OTCPK:OLYMY)(OTCPK:OCPNF) after a CEO change and Hoya (OTCPK:HOCPY). Although the investment case for industrial gases producer Nippon Sanso (OTCPK:TYNPF)(OTCPK:NPXYY) is progressing well, with improving margins due to self-help efficiency gains, we sold it to fund higher-upside positions.

As a result of rising tariff headwinds in North America, we exited our position in Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), whose freight rail network spans Canada, the U.S. and Mexico.

Outlook

International equities have long traded at a discount to their U.S. counterparts for fundamental reasons. Count among them a higher cost of capital, due to more stringent regulation, and less developed capital markets with which to fund the research and development in information and service industries. As part of Europe’s awakening to the reality of a changing global order, we believe these two areas will come into focus as drivers of further improvement and competitiveness. The current price for earnings growth disparity has created attractive growth opportunities for global investors seeking best-in-class substitutes for U.S. industry leaders, with the valuation disconnect most acute in the consumer sectors as well as financials (Exhibit 2).

Exhibit 2: Most Earnings Growth in Europe Comes Cheap

Innovation on average has been less prevalent across overseas markets than in the tech-centric U.S. economy. As growth investors with a wide mandate that targets companies at various stages of maturity and profitability, however, we can lean on our fundamental research to identify businesses thriving on their own merits. Our valuation approach to growth targets emerging growth companies harnessing innovation to rapidly develop into regional or global leaders like Sea Limited (SE) as well as secular growers expanding their market share in leading technologies like generative AI such as German software maker SAP (SAP).

Fund flows are another metric we study closely to gauge investor sentiment. After suffering years of capital drain to the U.S. market, Europe and Japan are seeing positive flows again. This trend is encouraging and could provide the impetus to turn the current reactionary, anti-U.S. trades into a more permanent shift of capital to the overseas regions we target.

Portfolio Highlights

During the first quarter, the ClearBridge International Growth ACWI Ex-US Strategy outperformed its MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Index benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy produced positive contributions across five of the eight sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total), with the financials, consumer discretionary and communication services sectors the top contributors and health care the main detractor.

On a relative basis, positive stock selection offset negative sector allocation effects. In particular, stock selection in the consumer discretionary, IT, financials, communication services and consumer staples sectors contributed to performance. Conversely, stock selection in the industrials, health care and materials sectors, an overweight to IT and an underweight to financials hurt results.

On a regional basis, stock selection in emerging markets and Asia Ex Japan, an underweight to emerging markets and an overweight to Europe Ex-U.K. proved beneficial while stock selection in Europe Ex-U.K. and an overweight to North America detracted from performance.

On an individual stock basis, the largest contributors to absolute returns in the quarter were BYD in consumer discretionary, Lloyds Banking Group (LYG)(OTCPK:LLDTF) and Deutsche Boerse (OTCPK:DBOEF)(OTCPK:DBOEY) in financials, as well as Sea Limited and Deutsche Telekom in communication services. The greatest detractors from absolute returns included positions in Novo Nordisk in health care, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELY)(OTCPK:TOELF) in IT as well as Trane Technologies and Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMF)(OTCPK:PRYMY) in industrials.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, the Strategy repurchased shares of Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY)(OTCPK:ADYYF) in financials and also added Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF)(OTCPK:SMNEY) in industrials, Galderma Group (OTCPK:GDERF)(OTCPK:GALDY) in health care as well as Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF) and Sika (OTCPK:SXYAY)(OTCPK:SKFOF) in materials. Exits included Haleon (HLN)(OTCPK:HLNCF) in consumer staples, Coupang (CPNG) and LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF)(OTCPK:LVMUY) in consumer discretionary, Xero (OTCPK:XROLF) in IT, Spotify (SPOT) in communication services and Deutsche Boerse in financials.

Elisa Mazen, Head of Global Growth, Portfolio Manager

Michael Testorf, CFA, Portfolio Manager

Pawel Wroblewski, CFA, Portfolio Manager

data.title: Commodities: Is Tariff Relief Real?
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Ewa Manthey

Energy - Tariff relief vs. Iranian talks

The oil market is quiet in early morning trading today, after settling lower for a second consecutive week last week. News that the Trump administration is offering tariff exemptions on certain electronics products initially supported risk assets. Since then, uncertainty returned as President Trump suggested exemptions are only temporary - and that other more specific tariffs could be introduced in due course. Meanwhile, market participants are digesting the implications of indirect weekend talks between the US and Iran, which were described as constructive. Further talks are planned. This may help remove some of the sanction risk affecting the oil market, particularly if talks keep on moving in the right direction.

Unsurprisingly, the latest positioning data shows that speculators reduced net longs in ICE Brent significantly over the last reporting week. Speculators sold 162,344 lots, leaving them with a net long of 155,838 lots as of last Tuesday. This was driven predominantly by longs liquidating. There also was also a small portion of new shorts entering the market. This marks the largest amount of speculative selling in a single week since at least 2015 - and by quite a distance. The move highlights the scale of the negative shift in market sentiment and the extent to which speculators were caught wrong-footed ahead of Trump’s tariff announcement on April 2.

Already, the weakness in the oil market appears to be causing a pullback in drilling activity in the US. The latest data from Baker Hughes shows that the US oil rig count fell by 9 last week, leaving it at 480. That’s the largest weekly decline since June 2023. Current West Texas Intermediate (WTI) prices leave little incentive for US producers to drill. Prices remaining near current levels could result in further declines in drilling activity.

On the data front, China will release its first batch of trade data for March today, including crude oil imports and trade in refined products. OPEC will also release its latest monthly oil market report today. The group has been slower to adjust its demand growth estimates. It will be interesting to see if recent tariff developments prompt OPEC to revise demand estimates lower.

Metals - Gold ETF buying

Recent reports suggest that Chinese gold exchange-traded funds (ETF) inflows reached a fresh daily record late last week. Investors continue to rush towards the yellow metal amid intensifying trade tensions. According to Bloomberg data, inflows into China’s four major gold ETFs reached a record of nearly 3 billion yuan (US$410M) on Thursday. However, speculative interest in Comex gold futures remains muted. Weekly Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) data show that managed money net longs in COMEX gold decreased a third consecutive week by 38,088 lots to 138,465 lots as of April 8. That’s the biggest weekly decline since October 3, 2023. Some of the selling could reflect meeting margin calls in other assets, given the recent volatility in broader markets.

Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) data shows that weekly inventories for all base metals fell over the reporting week. Copper led the decline, with stocks falling by 42,795 tonnes for a third consecutive week to 182,941 tonnes as of last Friday. This was the biggest weekly decline since April 2020, taking total inventories to the lowest since the end of January 2025. There are suggestions that domestic manufacturers and traders bought huge volumes of copper following the more recent weakness in prices. Meanwhile, aluminium inventories fell by 9,447 tonnes to 205,627 tonnes.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Vodafone: The Strain Behind The Yield
data.description:
Telecom giant Vodafone (NASDAQ:VOD) is a lucrative income play and has recently reported promising numbers for Q3 2025. Revenue growths were noted for the major UK market as well as in emerging markets like Turkey and Africa. There were some green shoots in emerging segments like IoT as well. However, there are some concerns around further growth in existing and emerging markets, and financial stability that needs to be evaluated.

Debt Issues Plague Growth Ambitions

Revenues have been on a stagnating path over the past few years, along with EBITDA. Dividend payout has been strong at a yield of 8.5%. While the dividend looks lucrative, the financial status backing the dividends is challenging. I do not like the free cash flow status of Vodafone, particularly in recent years. Utilizing cash reserves, asset sales and debt financing to meet dividend payouts are not strong signals for a long-term investor. Particularly given Vodafone's debt situation.

As of March 2024, Vodafone's debt stood at $58b. This is after a decrease from earlier, managed actively by reducing collateral liabilities, repayments and transfer of borrowing to discontinued operations. In some regions, like India, the path looks even more dire. Vodafone's joint venture with Idea Cellular in India reported a debt of $26b USD in Q3, 2024. This could lead to equity loss as the government recovers its spectrum auction dues by claiming close to 49% stake in the company. Elsewhere in Italy and Spain as well, Vodafone has been reducing debt by divesting stakes and asset sales.

Active management of debt looks great, but government and regulatory hurdles make it very difficult to predict the speed of resolution and expected outcomes. While improved cash flow, debt reduction and strategic focus on core business in the future could result after the dust settles, short-term dilution of revenue and a turbulent journey in the medium term is not ruled out. Vodafone will do its best to woo investors to ride this journey with robust dividend payouts no doubt, but as I said, drags in expected developments could alter the story here. The overall growth potential also looks diminished to me as stakes get reduced and businesses get wound up during the debt reduction drive.

While debt is commonplace for telecom companies (even AT&T or Verizon have commensurate debt for their sizes), Vodafone's exposure is across geographies and regulators and governments - which makes the case very challenging to manage. Additionally, AT&T and Verizon have higher margin businesses that give more room to play debt restructuring.

Is It a Good Time to Enter VOD?

No. The historical 5-year forward P/E multiple is 17.05, while it is currently trading at 22.17. Compare this with AT&T, currently a forward PE ratio of 12.63 and Verizon at 9.2, and it looks like VOD is not favorably valued at the moment. The stock is riding on speculation of a turnaround as the 6-month returns have not been impacted as well despite global equity turmoil.

No Solid Investment Thesis for Growth and Expansion

On the contrary, Vodafone is winding up operations in key segments and geographies. While that is great from a balance sheet perspective, it robs the firm of future growth potential in revenue terms. In Italy, VOD is selling its business to Swisscom. In Spain, VOD has sold its business to Zegona Communications. In India too, divestments talks with the government are ongoing.

These rollbacks dilute growth potential in areas VOD claims to focus on after reducing its debt obligations. Such as expanding its network in India and launching 5G services to stand up to fierce competition in the region. Growth in Africa, Turkey and Eastern Europe is also in play. Other futuristic investments in IoT and connected vehicles are also likely to face stiff competition. The theme is simple - when resources and energy are dragged into complex debt restructuring compared to competitors, growth initiatives suffer in a highly competitive market.

Impact of Tariff, Mergers and Acquisitions

Tariffs are not as great a risk as it is for some other global players. With a low US exposure, Vodafone will probably be hit by the tariff repercussions on equipment, indirectly at some point (depending on where it sources them from for each geography). However, since most of the trade is incoming, unless more retaliations unfold on the Trade War front, VOD should be considerably protected from huge repercussions. The export of network services to its trade partners are also not affected as of now.

On the M&A front, the biggest ongoing play is the merger with Three UK, which should unlock a larger market for VOD. The telecom sector is not averse to M&A developments that need to be evaluated for pros and cons on a case by case basis. The Three UK merger holds more promise than not, but further acquisitions and mergers in other areas will continuously pose risks to fundamental investors looking for long-term certainty. I usually play M&A not on news, but after it is effected (although it often means missing out on rallies at times) to ride over the regulatory uncertainty and implementation.

Hold VOD

Vodafone's nature of business, and particularly the debt situation it is in, doesn't make it an easy stock to recommend a BUY on. I remain concerned about ongoing spectrum license renewals that VOD will have to address despite stronger competition in a lot of geographies, as a major risk. Spectrum costs can erode margins even if VOD is successful despite its trailing financial position in some markets compared to peers. However, that is a concern for a longer-term investor, which I am.

In the medium term, dividends will remain under pressure, but will likely continue to be good in upcoming quarters. I rate VOD as a 'Hold' on this income play, along with the fact that the debt restructuring work will yield the expected gains at some point. Stiff competition and a notably trickier debt position compared to peers make its case difficult for new investments. New investments should only be considered as more clarity emerges, the Three UK merger is completed, or valuation opportunities arise due to corrections.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Garey J. Aitken, CFA & Timothy W. Caulfield, CFA

Metals, Defensives Support Canadian Resilience

Market Overview

Despite the first quarter of 2025 being dominated by uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariffs, a battle-hardened Canadian equity market, highlighted by sparkling performance in the materials sector, finished the quarter in positive territory, with the S&P/TSX Composite Total Return Index (TRI) increasing 1.5%. This was quite the achievement for the primary bourse of a country that’s been subject to a steady diet of threats by the current U.S. administration, including not only continuously changing tariffs (and vows to “crush” and “permanently shut down” the Canadian auto industry), but also Canada’s sovereignty. But make no mistake, the prospects of a global trade war weighed heavily on sentiment and most sectors were worse off for it. After the Canadian equity market reached an all-time high on January 30, it subsequently declined in both February and March while credit spreads meaningfully increased for the first time since 2022.

Tariffs and related measures are expected to slow the pace of economic activity — consumer spending, business activity, hiring and investing — while concurrently stoking inflation from higher costs. The economic impact for Canada could potentially be severe depending on the scope and duration of the trade war. The frustration of market participants around the world, including in the U.S. itself, was arguably predictable and exacerbated by the “self-infliction” of such heavy-handed policy. Despite strong opposition from many corners, tariffs have gone ahead all the same.

With outsize performance in materials, investors were reminded of the unique significance of the gold and silver sub-industries in the overall Canadian benchmark, accounting for a combined weighting of approximately 10% as of quarter end. These securities’ “safe haven” status and low correlation to outcomes for the broader market provided significant ballast in the first quarter in contrast to more cyclical and growth-oriented sectors. The benchmark heavy gold sub-industry advanced more than 34%, helped by a gold price increase of more than 18% to finish the quarter at an all-time high of US$3,123 per ounce.

Benchmark 10-year interest rates in Canada and the U.S. declined 26 basis points and 36 bps to finish at 2.97% and 4.21%, respectively. While the Federal Reserve stood pat in the first quarter, the Bank of Canada cut rates for the sixth and seventh consecutive times in January and March, ultimately to 2.75%, intending to boost consumption and housing. The outlook for further cuts is complicated by inflation concerns tied to tariffs. The Canadian dollar was flat against the U.S. dollar, ending the quarter at $1.44 CDN/USD.

Exhibit 1: Precious Metals, Commodities Lead Market Performance

On an absolute return basis, the Canadian equity market’s advance was narrow, with only four of 11 sectors producing gains: materials, utilities, energy and communication services. The energy sector was broadly solid, with crude oil prices relatively flat ending the quarter at US$71.48/bbl (West Texas Intermediate) and NYMEX natural gas prices increasing 13% to US$4.12/mmbtu (NYMEX). Resilience was shown by both the predictable utilities and washed-out communication services sectors. Health care and information technology (IT) were the worst-performing sectors.

Performance Overview

The ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy outperformed its benchmark in the first quarter with positive security selection more than offsetting weak sector allocation. An underweight to the exceptionally strong materials sector detracted, partially offset by an underweight to the underperforming financials and IT sectors, as well as our large overweight to the outperforming utilities sector. Security selection was positive and strongest in utilities, energy and financials.

In materials, underlying commodity price strength led to strong returns for Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Franco-Nevada (FNV), which the Strategy owns at an underweight to the benchmark.

In financials, Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD), the Strategy’s largest holding, materially outperformed the sector and all other banks, as its share price recovered from relative weakness in 2024 on anti-money-laundering fines, regulatory monitoring and asset restrictions. In addition to accelerating its CEO transition in January, TD announced in February that it would sell its entire equity investment in Charles Schwab and use the proceeds to buy back up to 5.7% of its outstanding shares, add over 100 bps to its already strong CET1 capital ratio, and invest in the business to drive organic growth.

In utilities, a large overweight to the sector and relative strength within it contributed to performance with the strength and predictability of regulated utility returns increasingly attractive in an uncertain environment. Utilities have benefited from lower interest rates, but their predictability seems equally, or more, important in the current environment.

Portfolio Positioning

With increased volatility and pockets of strength, trading activity in the first quarter of 2025 was more surgical, trimming select holdings on strength including a few defensive and more interest-rate-sensitive holdings such as Enbridge (ENB), Waste Connections (WCN), Metro (OTCPK:MTRAF)(OTCPK:MTTWF), ATCO (OTCPK:ACLLF), Hydro One (OTCPK:HRNNF) and Fortis (FTS). We also trimmed a few of the market’s deemed cyclical winners, such as Atkins-Realis (OTCPK:SNCAF), Manulife (MFC) and TMX Group (OTCPK:TMXXF). Meanwhile, buying centered around out-of-favor cyclical names Alimentation Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), Canadian National Railway (CNI) as well as BCE (BCE) and Shopify (SHOP). Additions also included two new holdings: Teck Resources (TECK) and Intact Financial (IFC)(OTCPK:IFCZF). While the Strategy is typically low turnover, we will continue to be decisive when warranted and as further opportunities arise.

Recent weakness in the shares offered an attractive opportunity to buy Teck Resources in materials. In recent years, Teck has completed a major transformation into a pure-play copper growth company, having disposed of its oil sands and coal businesses in 2023 and 2024, respectively. Following these dispositions, Teck has an exceptionally strong financial position with ample cash on the balance sheet and is in the midst of returning meaningful capital to shareholders. Having completed the construction of its (60%-owned) giant Quebrada Blanca (QB) copper mine in 2024, Teck is focused on growing its copper production meaningfully in the coming years. Free cash flow is inflecting positively as QB kicks in. While demand is being impacted by geopolitical and trade tensions in the world, persistent supply challenges also characterize copper market fundamentals, to Teck’s benefit.

New addition IFC has illustrated its past success with its 20% market share in Canada, an average return on equity outperformance of 500+ bps versus the industry and high-single-digit compounding of profitability over the long term. IFC’s strengths are rooted in superior pricing and risk selection, claims expertise, a solid M&A track record and strong investment management contributions.

In IT, we continued to build our position in Shopify as recent results continue to illuminate the improved financial stability and capital allocation mindset guiding the business. The ever-expanding total addressable market, robust runway of embedded margin expansion and pricing power, improved capital allocation, and a best-in-class balance sheet continue to unlock the intrinsic value of the business at a rapid clip.

At quarter end, the Strategy’s largest sector exposures were financials, industrials and energy. Relative to the benchmark, the Strategy is overweight the generally defensive/non-cyclical consumer staples and utilities sectors, as well as industrials. The Strategy is most underweight the typically value-oriented/cyclical financials, materials and energy sectors.

Outlook

For Canada, concerns surrounding the likes of housing affordability and improvements in productivity have expanded to include the scope, magnitude and duration of tariffs and a host of related issues — for instance, diversifying export markets, including for Canadian energy. Developments of recent months are undoubtedly a net negative for the economy with “better-than-feared” tariff treatment by the U.S. administration so far the consolation prize. Canadian business and consumer confidence are weak. For business investment in Canada this could be setting up to be a dark period as commitments to long-term capital allocation decisions could be deemed too difficult to make.

For investors in Canadian equities, which in many cases have reach well beyond Canada’s borders, the overall outlook has been muddied. Global economic uncertainty has materially increased, new downside risks have emerged, and the rationale for the U.S. administration plunging the world into a trade war is at best unclear at the outset. In managing the ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy, we are always working with different degrees of imperfect information about the future; however, at present both transparency and visibility are lacking. This unsurprisingly leads to risk aversion in equities. Hopes that haphazard policies in the U.S. would ultimately wind up being more hyperbole and negotiating tactics have been dashed for the time being.

Through periods of turmoil, such as the one we are currently navigating, it is particularly important to maintain a long-term perspective, resisting myopia in the moment. For instance, Canada has a comparative advantage in the production of raw materials, including energy, and this has not changed. Canada is the number one export market for 34 U.S. states, often with deeply integrated ties.

Furthermore, the interconnectedness of the two countries is even more evident when considering that much of this trade is between parties with cross ownership. Global impacts and second-order effects will be critical. However, economically destructive policies at minimum provide strong incentives to find a resolution. And while cooperation is likely too valuable to ignore over time, tariffs and retaliatory measures will likely result in higher near-term inflation, inflicting a heavy “tax” as businesses make suboptimal decisions. Make no mistake; some damage is done. However, Canada’s economy is, and will remain, heavily intertwined with the U.S., as will many of the businesses owned in the Strategy.

Without compromising on valuation, owning businesses with resilience and predictability remains our preferred positioning. We like these businesses along with those facing challenges and uncertainties where a lower share price is already discounting the increased risk in the environment. When risks are heightened, probabilistic thinking becomes even more valuable in evaluating the range and likelihood of various future scenarios, directly impacting risk/reward assessments. Typical concurrent volatility in these circumstances can present exceptional opportunities. Our positioning can provide ballast in more challenging equity market environments, allows the Strategy to power ahead with predictable growth, and serves as dry powder when better risk/reward opportunities arise.

Portfolio Highlights

During the first quarter, the ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy outperformed its S&P/TSX benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy generated gains in five of the 10 sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total). The primary contributors were the materials, energy and utilities sectors while the main detractor was the IT sector.

Relative to the benchmark, security selection was positive and strongest in utilities, energy and financials, more than offsetting negative sector allocation. An underweight to the exceptionally strong materials sector detracted, partially offset by an underweight to the underperforming financials and IT sectors as well as our large overweight to the outperforming utilities sector.

On an individual stock basis, leading absolute contributors included Franco-Nevada, Toronto-Dominion Bank, Agnico Eagle Mines, Fortis and Waste Connections. Top absolute detractors included Alimentation Couche-Tard, Brookfield Corporation (BN), Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), Royal Bank of Canada (RY) and Shopify.

Garey J. Aitken, CFA , Head of Canadian Equities

Timothy W. Caulfield, CFA, Dir. Canadian Eq. Research

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Copyright © 2025 ClearBridge Investments. data.title: ClearBridge Canadian Small Cap Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Garey J. Aitken, CFA & Michael Richmond, CFA

Gold Rush Carries Small Caps

Market Overview

We suspect market participants from a century ago would have felt right at home with the topics that dominated Canadian small cap equities in the first quarter of 2025 — tariffs and gold. Tariffs were by far the most impactful macro driver of equity performance. The “will-they, won’t-they” gyrations out of Washington resulted in a sell first, ask questions later approach to a broad swath of the Canadian small cap market.

Equities with even the faintest ties to either U.S. trade or the broader North American economy sold off during the quarter. The cyclically exposed small cap information technology (IT), industrials, financials and energy sectors all trailed the S&P/TSX Small Cap Index and the S&P/TSX Composite Index, while defensive sectors such as consumer staples, utilities, real estate and gold producers within materials outperformed the benchmark.

For the most part, we have yet to see a material change in operating environments or forward outlooks for most cyclical equities. Nonetheless, market participants were clearly responding to the uncertainty around tariffs — both their intensity and duration — during the quarter and the corresponding uncertainty around the broader economy.

Offsetting the cyclical weakness in the small cap market during the first quarter was a sharp move higher in gold prices. Gold closed the quarter at an all-time high of U.S. $3,123/oz — up 19% for the quarter and 39% year over year. Central bank buying and the perceived safety has pushed gold steadily higher. The heavily gold-weighted small cap materials sector followed suit, gaining 19% overall during the first quarter, with the gold sub-industry advancing a substantial 38%.

The net result was a flat, but volatile, quarter for the S&P/TSX Small Cap Total Return Index. The index was up 0.9% during the quarter versus the 1.5% return for the large cap S&P/TSX Composite Total Return Index and a 9.6% decline in CAD for the Russell 2000 — an index of U.S. small cap equities. The heavy weighting toward gold equities drove the relative outperformance of the Canadian small cap index. Ex-materials, the S&P/TSX Small Cap TRI was down 5% for the quarter.

Despite the volatile geopolitical backdrop, most macro variables were benign to start the year. The Canadian 10-year yield dropped by just 26 bps to 2.97%, and the spread to the U.S. 10-year yield tightened by 10 bps as the U.S. 10-year declined by 36 bps to 4.21%. The Canadian dollar was effectively unchanged around $1.44 CDN/USD. The West Texas Intermediate crude price was also flat, ending the quarter at approximately U.S. $71.50/bbl.

Exhibit 1: Sector Performance Varied Widely

The ClearBridge Canadian Small Cap Strategy underperformed its benchmark by ~500 bps for the quarter, with our underweight allocation to materials, particularly gold, being the primary source of the underperformance. The portfolio was also negatively impacted by underperformance in financials, energy and an overweight allocation to industrials, while relative performance was supported by IT and utilities.

Portfolio Positioning

Volatility and M&A activity allowed us to be active during the quarter. We eliminated two equities following takeout offers, added two new names to the portfolio, and were vigorous in adding to positions where we believe the market is being overly punitive.

We eliminated Canadian Western Bank following its completed combination with National Bank Financial and Converge Technology Solutions (OTCQX:CTSDF) following its announced take private transaction.

We added North American Construction Group (NOA) and Osisko Gold Royalties (OR) to the portfolio. NOA is a mining services and civil construction company that provides mine services to the Canadian oil sands (25% of revenue), Australian coal and copper mines (55%) and various civil projects, with a long history of steady, profitable growth. We believe the equity is trading at an attractive discount to intrinsic value based on unfounded concerns around contract structure in the oil sands and idle Canadian assets, which we believe can be profitably redeployed to their newly acquired Australian business.

In the case of Osisko Royalties, we have followed the name for a long time and now view the governance issues that had previously kept us on the sidelines to be behind the company. In addition, the valuation was attractive as it had materially lagged the small/mid cap gold royalties, creating a compelling entry point.

We were energetic in adding to industrials, materials, financials and energy names during the quarter at attractive discounts to intrinsic value as the market shifted away from more cyclical areas. We added to ATS Corp (ATS), Colliers International (CIGI), Propel Holdings (OTCPK:PRLPF), Calian Group (OTCPK:CLNFF), Richelieu Hardware (OTCPK:RHUHF), Methanex (MEOH) and Adentra (OTCPK:HDIUF). We view these as a collection of high-quality businesses that have been negatively affected by the broader market selloff.

We reduced our position in Trican Well Service (OTCPK:TOLWF) early in the quarter on strength and ATCO (OTCPK:ACLLF) following a strong run in the equity.

Outlook

Our investment approach is a bottom-up strategy that prioritizes identifying and capitalizing on market inefficiencies, using our proprietary research, and the time arbitrage that comes with our long-term investment horizon. This approach is supported by our patient culture, enabling us to make well-informed decisions when there are discrepancies between expectations and underlying fundamentals.

We remain steadfast in our commitment to our investment style, diligently seeking out businesses that exhibit wise capital allocation practices, maintain structural competitive advantages and can generate high-quality growth — all under appropriate capital structures.

The Trump administration’s “Liberation Day” tariffs shortly after the quarter end confirmed many of the worst fears of the market. While Canada seems to have been spared the most draconian of the U.S. tariff plans, the size and unknown duration of its tariff agenda has justifiably brought into question the outlook for inflation, employment and GDP growth globally.

Canadian market participants will have a lot to consider over the coming months: the implications of the tariffs as they stand today, a broadly more protectionist southern neighbor and a new government in Ottawa. Regardless how events unfold we believe that high-quality growth companies — with appropriate capital allocation policies, capital structures and durable competitive advantages that produce above-average returns — should be well prepared to weather the uncertainty. Moreover, we believe that these companies should be well-positioned to be proactive in improving their competitive positions in volatile markets.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Canadian Small Cap Strategy underperformed its benchmark in the quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy generated gains in three of the nine sectors in which it was invested (11 total). The primary detractors from absolute returns were the cyclical financials, industrials and energy sectors while the utilities and IT sectors were the main positive contributors to absolute performance.

Relative to the benchmark, sector allocation and security selection were both negative. The Strategy was negatively impacted by an underweight position to materials and an overweight to IT, industrials, and financials. Selection was most negative in materials, utilities and financials. On the positive side, selection in the IT and industrials sectors as well as our single health care holding, DRI Healthcare Trust (OTC:DHTRF), contributed to relative results.

Among individual securities, the leading absolute contributors were Converge Technology Solutions in IT, Sandstorm Gold (SAND) in materials, AltaGas (OTCPK:ATGFF) in utilities, Empire in consumer staples and Interrent REIT (OTC:IIPZF) in real estate. The largest drags to absolute performance were Propel Holdings (OTCPK:PRLPF) in financials, Enerflex (EFXT) in energy, Methanex in materials, Adentra in industrials and Jamieson Wellness (OTCPK:JWLLF) in consumer staples.

Garey J. Aitken, CFA, Head of Canadian Equities

Michael Richmond, CFA, Portfolio Manager

data.title: GBIL: A Recession-Resilient ETF
data.description:
Thesis

One of the most surprising aspects of the recent market turmoil is the volatility associated with many short-term funds, instruments which were supposed to be equivalent to cash parking vehicles. Investors have found out the hard way that the only safe harbor during a recession or market sell-off is represented by T-Bills.

In today's article, we are going to analyze the Goldman Sachs Access Treasury 0-1 Year ETF (NYSEARCA:GBIL), a fund which we last covered almost three years ago. We are going to show investors why the name has a robust build for today's challenging markets, and why its duration profile is favorable.

When the Storm Comes, Not All Cash Parking Funds Move Equally

Let us have a look at a cohort of short-term funds, and see how they behaved during the recent market sell-off:

We are comparing pure T-Bill funds such as GBIL, iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO) and SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) to AAA CLO names such as the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) and short-term bond funds such as the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT) and the Janus Henderson Short Duration Income ETF (VNLA). Lastly, we have the Neos Enhanced Income 1-3 Month ETF (CSHI) which layers put spreads in order to enhance the dividend yield.

We have covered some of these names in the past, and you can find our research here:

  • TFLO: In An Uncertain World, Floating Rate Treasuries Deliver
  • BIL: Ideal Fund For Today's Environment, Over 5% Yield
  • JAAA: 'Higher For Longer' Rates Favor This CLO Fund

As we can observe from the above graph, JAAA and CSHI are by far the most volatile names, with drawdowns of -1.5%. The best performer is GBIL, followed by BIL, both treasury only funds. The rest of the cohort has modestly negative returns.

As an investor, you want positive returns during market turmoil. Cash parking vehicles are supposed to be safe heavens, and even if the drawdown is very small, it is unwarranted. The recent market action has shown us that pure treasury funds (we are using this term to include T-Bills) are the best choice during such volatile times.

The reason is simple: duration and no credit risk. JAAA has credit risk (albeit small), CSHI contains equity risk (again, a small one), VNLA and MINT also run credit risk. When the worst storm comes, credit risk will manifest itself even in the safest short-term bond funds, but not for treasuries.

Duration is Your Friend in Today's Environment

In today's environment when Fed cuts are expected, you want to have a fund with a longer duration. GBIL fits that bill with a 0.34 years option adjusted duration, and a 0.35 years weighted average maturity. The fund comes with a 0.12% net expense ratio and the Goldman expertise.

The fund contains only 39 holdings, which are all T-Bills. This translates into no credit risk for the fund, as we have seen during the recent market turmoil. The current 30-day SEC yield is 4.15%, and the fund maintains a constant NAV:

We like the fact that the fund has a NAV of 100 because it is optically very easy to identify where one's investment stands. GBIL does exactly what it is supposed to do, namely maintain a constant NAV while delivering a money market yield. Even when the equity markets are down -10% or -20%, the fund maintains its value, which is exactly how a cash parking vehicle is supposed to behave.

Fund Analytics

  • AUM: $6.3 billion
  • Expense Ratio: 0.12%
  • Year Range NAV: 99.66–100.30
  • St Dev: 0.5%
  • Composition: T-Bills

Yield Enhancement and Its Drawbacks

While GBIL is robust and reliable, it functions as a harvester of short term T-Bill yields, thus might be considered 'boring'. In the past few years we have seen the advent of 'yield enhancement' funds which use options to provide for higher dividend yields. These instruments are exciting in normalized environments since they do offer increased dividends, but are a bit of a headache during sell-offs. As we saw from the above chart, CSHI saw a very significant drawdown, and is still negative for the month.

Similarly, funds like JAAA which pay higher dividends because of the illiquid nature of their holdings, also saw significant drawdowns, making them less palatable for some investors. The take-away here is that there is no 'extra' yield without some sort of drawback. The drawback is represented by a higher level of volatility, and the possibility of having a prolonged period to get back to NAV.

Conclusion

GBIL is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name invests in short-dated T-bills, with a portfolio duration of 0.34 years. The ETF lacks any credit risk via its composition, and has done tremendously well in the past month when many peers have had drawdowns. An investor needs to keep in mind that any yield enhancement vehicle comes with additional risks, be it liquidity or equity risk. We have seen such vehicles experience drawdowns during the recent market moves, but not for GBIL. We find this name suitable for a recession and are a 'Strong Buy' in today's macro environment.
